_meta.what_firm_does: Harvey is an AI company building domain-specific generative AI for legal and professional-services work — law firms, in-house teams, and professional-services providers — to automate research, drafting, and analysis. Mission framed as transforming professional services.
_meta.jd_wants: An experienced People Business Partner (JD targets ~6–10 years) to partner with GTM leaders — coaching managers, surfacing org themes early, handling employee relations, and shaping people strategy for fast-moving teams.
_meta.status: DRAFT — Jamie to review/edit before submit

Why are you interested in this role?: Being a People Business Partner isn't just about resolving people issues as they come—from my experience, it's about staying close enough to the business to surface themes early, coaching leaders through growth, and partnering on the people strategy that lets fast-moving teams perform. At Vestas Wind Systems I partnered directly with the HRBP team on employee relations, headcount, and succession planning, and helped coach 12 senior leaders through an Inclusive Leadership pilot that later scaled to 23 global locations. That work taught me what it means to advise leaders, not just administer HR. Harvey's mission to transform professional services — and a People team where judgment matters more than perfect information — are genuine draws.
Tell us about a time you partnered with: At NextGen Healthcare I led a manager-effectiveness initiative where leaders sensed engagement was slipping but couldn't say why. I analyzed 2,000+ employee experience responses to find what was actually driving it, then turned that into reports that surfaced the themes and risks for leadership — which helped the C-suite prioritize manager training as the key lever. Rather than bring leaders a complaint, I brought them a diagnosis and a recommendation. That taught me that the most useful thing a People partner does is diagnose early and bring data to the development conversation, so leaders can act before a problem becomes attrition.
How do you handle a difficult employee r: At Vestas I assisted on employee relations cases and learned to balance genuine empathy for the individual with what's right for the team — while holding compliance and confidentiality carefully. My instinct is to slow down enough to understand the actual pattern before reacting: is this one situation, or a signal of something systemic? My org-psych training pushes me to treat it as a system, not just an incident. I'll be candid about where I am: I've supported and co-owned these cases rather than carried a full senior caseload solo, so I'd be growing into the depth this role expects — but the judgment, discretion, and care are already how I work.
Anything else you'd like us to know? (ad: I'll be direct — this JD leans senior, and on paper I sit earlier in my career than the 6–10 years it describes. I'd rather name that than paper over it. What I'd bring is the actual substance of the work done at real scale: HRBP partnership on employee relations and succession at Vestas, people-data diagnosis for the C-suite at NextGen, and cross-functional coaching with Sales, Marketing, and Tech leaders at InGenius through fast change. My approach is grounded in my MS in Applied Organizational Psychology (USC): I diagnose with data, collaborate cross-functionally, and track whether it worked. As an analytical organizer with a growth mindset, I'd love to partner with your GTM leaders and grow into the full scope.
